title: Minimum Number of Coins for Fruits
content:
You are given an 0-indexed integer array prices where prices[i] denotes the number of coins needed to purchase the (i + 1)th fruit.

The fruit market has the following reward for each fruit:

• If you purchase the (i + 1)th fruit at prices[i] coins, you can get any number of the next i fruits for free.

Note that even if you can take fruit j for free, you can still purchase it for prices[j - 1] coins to receive its reward.

Return the minimum number of coins needed to acquire all the fruits.

Example 1:

Input: prices = [3,1,2]

Output: 4

Explanation:

• Purchase the 1st fruit with prices[0] = 3 coins, you are allowed to take the 2nd fruit for free.

• Purchase the 2nd fruit with prices[1] = 1 coin, you are allowed to take the 3rd fruit for free.

• Take the 3rd fruit for free.

Note that even though you could take the 2nd fruit for free as a reward of buying 1st fruit, you purchase it to receive its reward, which is more optimal.

Example 2:

Input: prices = [1,10,1,1]

Output: 2

Explanation:

• Purchase the 1st fruit with prices[0] = 1 coin, you are allowed to take the 2nd fruit for free.

• Take the 2nd fruit for free.

• Purchase the 3rd fruit for prices[2] = 1 coin, you are allowed to take the 4th fruit for free.

• Take the 4th fruit for free.

Example 3:

Input: prices = [26,18,6,12,49,7,45,45]

Output: 39

Explanation:

• Purchase the 1st fruit with prices[0] = 26 coin, you are allowed to take the 2nd fruit for free.

• Take the 2nd fruit for free.

• Purchase the 3rd fruit for prices[2] = 6 coin, you are allowed to take the 4th, 5th and 6th (the next three) fruits for free.

• Take the 4th fruit for free.

• Take the 5th fruit for free.

• Purchase the 6th fruit with prices[5] = 7 coin, you are allowed to take the 8th and 9th fruit for free.

• Take the 7th fruit for free.

• Take the 8th fruit for free.

Note that even though you could take the 6th fruit for free as a reward of buying 3rd fruit, you purchase it to receive its reward, which is more optimal.

Constraints:

• 1 <= prices.length <= 1000

• 1 <= prices[i] <= 105
hints: The intended solution uses Dynamic Programming.
Let dp[i] denote the minimum number of coins, such that we bought i<sup>th</sup> fruit and acquired all the fruits in the range [i...n].
dp[i] = min(dp[i], dp[j] + prices[i]) , where j is in the range [i + 1, i + 1 + i].

solutions.python:
from collections import deque

class Solution(object):
    def minimumCoins(self, prices):
        n = len(prices)
        # dp[i] = min coins to acquire all fruits from index i to n-1 (1-indexed internally)
        # Buying fruit i+1 (0-indexed: i) at prices[i] gives free access to next i fruits
        # i.e., free fruits i+1 .. 2i+1 (0-indexed: i+1 .. 2i+1)
        # So from index i (0-indexed), we can next buy any fruit j in [i+1+1, i+1+i+1] = [i+2, 2i+2]
        # dp[i] = prices[i] + min(dp[j]) for j in [i+1, 2*i+1] (0-indexed next purchases)
        # Base: dp[n-1] = prices[n-1]

        # Work backwards with a monotone deque for range minimum
        dp = [0] * (n + 1)
        dq = deque()  # stores indices in increasing order with increasing dp values

        for i in range(n - 1, -1, -1):
            # dp[i] = prices[i] + min(dp[i+1 .. 2i+2]) or just prices[i] if 2i+2 >= n
            # free range: fruits i+2 to 2i+2 (0-indexed), or all remaining if beyond n
            # We can jump to buy any fruit from index i+1 to min(2i+1, n-1) next (0-indexed)
            # But we must acquire ALL fruits, so we buy next fruit somewhere in [i+1, 2i+1]

            # Remove from deque indices outside the valid range [i+1, 2*(i+1)]
            # Valid next purchase: j in [i+1, 2*i+1] (0-indexed), so dp values dp[i+1..2i+2]
            right = 2 * (i + 1)  # inclusive right bound (0-indexed)
            # Remove indices outside range from front
            while dq and dq[0] < i + 1:
                dq.popleft()

            # dp[i] = prices[i] + min in window
            if not dq:
                dp[i] = prices[i]  # no more fruits needed after buying this
            else:
                dp[i] = prices[i] + dp[dq[0]]

            # Add i to deque (maintain increasing dp order)
            while dq and dp[dq[-1]] >= dp[i]:
                dq.pop()
            if i <= right:  # only add if i can be a valid next step for some earlier index
                dq.append(i)

        # Actually simpler O(n^2) DP for n<=1000:
        dp2 = [float('inf')] * (n + 1)
        dp2[n] = 0
        for i in range(n - 1, -1, -1):
            # Buying fruit i (0-indexed) gives free fruits i+1 .. 2i+1
            # Next purchase can be at j in [i+1, 2i+2] (the first NOT free fruit)
            # But if 2i+2 >= n, we don't need to buy anything more
            lo = i + 1
            hi = min(2 * i + 2, n)  # exclusive
            dp2[i] = prices[i] + min(dp2[lo:hi+1])
        return dp2[0]
solutions.explanation:
DP where dp[i] = minimum coins to acquire all fruits starting from index i (0-indexed). Buying fruit i costs prices[i] and makes fruits i+1 through 2i+1 free (you can skip buying them, or buy them for extra rewards).

From index i, the next fruit we must purchase is somewhere in [i+1, 2i+2] (the first fruit outside the free window). dp[i] = prices[i] + min(dp[i+1..2i+2]).

For n ≤ 1000 an O(n²) solution is fast enough.

Time: O(n²). Space: O(n).
